symbols: kneeling elderly king — worldly power bowing before divine infancy
gifts of gold — kingship offered to the King of Kings
ruined classical architecture — the old order crumbling as the new kingdom is born
star in the sky — the celestial sign that guided the Gentile seekers to Christ
dark-skinned Magus — the universality of Christ's kingship extending to all nations
globe held by the African Magus — the whole world brought as offering to the newborn King
description: A richly detailed scene of the Adoration of the Magi set amid elaborate ruined classical architecture with crumbling columns, broken walls, and overgrown vegetation. At the left, the Virgin Mary in a blue mantle holds the Christ Child on her lap. Before them, an elderly king in a rich dark robe kneels, offering a golden vessel. Behind him stand two other kings — one middle-aged, the other a dark-skinned man in red holding a golden globe. A faint star shines in the dark sky above. The architectural ruins create a dramatic backdrop of decaying grandeur.
theological_description: The Adoration of the Magi (Matthew 2:1-12) is the Epiphany — the revelation of Christ to the Gentile nations. The Magi, scholars from the East guided by a star, represent the first fruits of the universal harvest of souls that Christ's birth inaugurates. Their three gifts carry profound symbolism: gold for kingship, frankincense for divinity, and myrrh for the burial of the mortal body. The ruined classical architecture is a deliberate theological statement: the old pagan world crumbles while the new creation begins in an infant's lap. The inclusion of a dark-skinned Magus, representing Africa, fulfills the prophecy of Psalm 72:10-11 that kings from every nation would bring gifts and bow before the Messiah. The Magi's journey from East to West, following a star to find a child, inverts all worldly wisdom: the learned seek knowledge and find it not in libraries but in a manger.

scripture_refs.reason: The visit of the Magi from the East, guided by the star, bearing gifts — the exact scene depicted
Kings of Tarshish and the isles bring gifts, kings of Sheba and Seba offer tribute — all kings bow before the Messiah
Nations shall come to your light and kings to the brightness of your rising, bearing gold and frankincense
The Gentiles are fellow heirs — the Magi as the first Gentile worshippers of Christ
